prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response: You're probably wrong about how often other people think about you. Many of us have a tendency to believe we're more noticeable to others than we actually are - psychologists call this the "spotlight effect." While you might ruminate over that awkward thing you said or worry about how you came across in a meeting, most people are too focused on their own concerns to remember these details about you. This isn't because people don't care about you, but simply because everyone is the main character in their own story. It can be both humbling and liberating to realize we occupy much less space in others' thoughts than we imagine.
